subject: Chemistry
q: The pH of a $0.1$ molar solution of the acid $\mathrm{HQ}$ is $3$ . The value of the ionization constant, Ka of this acid is :
question: The pH of a $0.1$ molar solution of the acid $\mathrm{HQ}$ is $3$ . The value of the ionization constant, Ka of this acid is :
solution:
$\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \mathrm{C}} \Rightarrow 10^{-3}=\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}} \cdot 10^{-1}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=10^{-5}$

solution:

subject: Chemistry
q:
What would be the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a solution obtained by mixing $5 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetic acid and $7.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of sodium acetate and making the volume equal to $500 \mathrm{~mL}$ ? 
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=1.75 \times 10^{-5}, \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=4.76\right)$
question:
What would be the $\mathrm{pH}$ of a solution obtained by mixing $5 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetic acid and $7.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of sodium acetate and making the volume equal to $500 \mathrm{~mL}$ ? 
$\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=1.75 \times 10^{-5}, \mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}=4.76\right)$
options: $\mathrm{pH}$ of solution will be equal to $\mathrm{pH}$ of acetic acid
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{pH} & =\mathrm{pK}_a+\log \frac{[\text { salt }]}{[\text { acid }]} \\
& =4.76+\log \frac{\frac{7.5}{500}}{\frac{5}{500}}=4.7+\log 1.5=4.87
\end{aligned}$
Hence correct answer is $4.76 < \mathrm{pH} < 5.0$

subject: Chemistry
q: 0.01 moles of a weak acid HA Ka=2.0×10-6 is dissolved in 1.0 L of 0.1M HCl solution. The degree of dissociation of HA is __________ ×10-5 (Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Neglect volume change on adding HA and assume degree of dissociation <<1]
question: 0.01 moles of a weak acid HA Ka=2.0×10-6 is dissolved in 1.0 L of 0.1M HCl solution. The degree of dissociation of HA is __________ ×10-5 (Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Neglect volume change on adding HA and assume degree of dissociation <<1]
solution:
HA        ⇌            H+               +   A-Initial conc.          0.1M0M           Equ. conc. (0.01-x)             (0.1+x)xM≈0.01M               ≈0.1M

Now, Ka=H+A-[HA]⇒2×10-6=0.1×x0.01

∴x=2×10-7

Now, α=x0.01=2×10-70.01=2×10-5

subject: Chemistry
q: An analyst wants to convert 1 L HCl of pH=1 to a solution of HCl of pH=2. The volume of water needed to do this dilution is _____ mL. (Nearest integer)
question: An analyst wants to convert 1 L HCl of pH=1 to a solution of HCl of pH=2. The volume of water needed to do this dilution is _____ mL. (Nearest integer)
solution:
The concentration of H+ or say HCl in both the given solution is 10–1 and 10–2 mole L–1 respectively.

For the given amount of HCl the concentration depends over dilution hence

M1V1=M2V2M1M2=V2V1

10-110-2=V21L

V2=10 L=10000 mL

∆V=10000-1000 mL=9000 mL

subject: Chemistry
q: $\mathrm{NaOH}$ is a strong base. What will be $\mathrm{pH}$ of $5.0 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$ solution ? $(\log 2=0.3)$
question: $\mathrm{NaOH}$ is a strong base. What will be $\mathrm{pH}$ of $5.0 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$ solution ? $(\log 2=0.3)$
solution:
Given $\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]=5 \times 10^{-2}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \mathrm{pOH}=-\log 5 \times 10^{-2} \\
& \quad \quad=-\log 5+2 \log 10=1.30 \\
& \because \mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH}=14 \\
& \because \mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH} \\
& \quad=14-1.30=12.70
\end{aligned}
$$

q: The solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CdSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> in water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Its solubility in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> solution is ___ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> (Round off to the Nearest integer) (Assume that solubility is much less than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> )
question: The solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CdSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> in water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Its solubility in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> solution is ___ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> (Round off to the Nearest integer) (Assume that solubility is much less than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> )
solution:
In pure water,

Ksp=S2=8×10-42

=64×10-8

In 0.01 M H2SO4

H2SO4(aq)x→2H(aq)+x+SO42-(aq.)(x+0.01)

Ksp=x(x+0.01)

=64×10-8

x+0.01≅0.01 M

So, x(0.01)=64×10-8

x=64×10-6M

pH=7+12pka-pkb

=7+125.23-4.75

=7.24≈7

The solubility of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Bi</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>molL</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>298</mn><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mrow></math> is

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following mixture gives a buffer solution with $\mathrm{pH}=9.25$ ?
Given : $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right)=4.75$
question:
Which of the following mixture gives a buffer solution with $\mathrm{pH}=9.25$ ?
Given : $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{OH}\right)=4.75$
solution:
$pOH = pK_b + \log \dfrac{Salt}{Base}$
$4.75 = 4.75 + \log \dfrac{Salt}{Base}$
Milimoles of [Salt] = milimoles of [Base]
$NH_4OH + HCl \rightarrow NH_4Cl + H_2O$
$0.2M, 0.5L \quad 0.1M, 0.5L$
$100 \text{ mmole} \quad 50 \text{ mmole}$
$50 \text{ mmole} \quad - \quad 50 \text{ mmole}$
Milimoles of $NH_4OH$ = milimoles of $NH_4Cl$

subject: Chemistry
q: Sulphurous acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ka</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ka</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>588</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is____________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>Round off to the Nearest Integer<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>)</mo></math>.
question: Sulphurous acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ka</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Ka</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>588</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is____________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>Round off to the Nearest Integer<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>)</mo></math>.
solution:
H2SO3 [Dibasic acid]

c=0.588 M

⇒ pH of solution is due to First dissociation only since Ka1>>Ka2

⇒ First dissociation of H2SO3

H2SO3(aq)⇌H⊕(aq)+HSO3-(aq) : ka1=1.7×10-2

t=      0

t       C-x              x                x

⇒Ka1=1.7100=H⊕HSO3-H2SO3

⇒1.7100=x20.588-x

⇒1.7×0.588-1.7x=100x2

⇒100x2+1.7x-1=0

⇒H⊕=x=-1.7+(1.7)2+4×100×12×100=0.09186

Therefore pH of sol. is :

pH=-logH⊕

⇒pH=-log0.09186=1.036≃1

If the solubility product of AB2 is 3.20×10-11 M3, then the solubility of AB2 in pure water is _______ ×10-4 mol L-1

question:

[Assuming that neither kind of ion reacts with water]
solution:
AB2⇌A2++2B−s2s

Ksp=4s3=3.20×10−11

So solubility =2×10−4 mol L−1

subject: Chemistry
q: pH of water is 7 at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If water is heated to $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$., it's pH will :
question: pH of water is 7 at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If water is heated to $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$., it's pH will :
solution:
At $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, pure water has $\mathrm{pH}=7$
As temperature increased, water molecules dissociate more into hydrogen ions ( $\mathrm{H}^{+}$) and hydroxide ions $\left(\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)$.
This increased dissociation leads to slightly decrease in pH .
At $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{pH} \approx 6.93$

question: Which of the following is a Lewis acid?
solution: As boron has vacant p-orbital (electron-deficient species), it will behave like a Lewis acid.

subject: Chemistry
q: The solubility (in mol $\mathrm{L}^{-1}$ ) of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ $\left(K_{\mathrm{sp}}=1.0 \times 10^{-10}\right)$ in a $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KCl}$ solution will be
question: The solubility (in mol $\mathrm{L}^{-1}$ ) of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ $\left(K_{\mathrm{sp}}=1.0 \times 10^{-10}\right)$ in a $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KCl}$ solution will be
solution:
Let solubility of $\mathrm{AgCl}=x \mathrm{~mole} / \mathrm{L}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{AgCl} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\
& \text {i.e., } K_{\mathrm{sp}(\mathrm{AgCl})}=x \times x \\
& \mathrm{KCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\
& {\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right] \text {from } \mathrm{KCl}=0.1 \mathrm{~m}} \\
& \text { Total }\left[\mathrm{Cl}{ }^{-}\right] \text {in solution }=x+0.1 \\
& K_{s p}(\mathrm{AgCl})=\left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]=x(x+0.1) \\
& 1.0 \times 10^{-10}=x(x+0.1) \\
& 1.0 \times 10^{-10}=x^2+0.1 x \\
& 1.0 \times 10^{-10}=0.1 x \quad\left(\text { as } x^2< < 1\right) \\
& x=1.0 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q: The pH of a 0.01 M weak acid $\mathrm{HX}\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=4 \times 10^{-10}\right)$ is found to be 5. Now the acid solution is diluted with excess of water so that the pH of the solution changes to 6. The new concentration of the diluted weak acid is given as $x \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$. The value of x is ________ (nearest integer)
question: The pH of a 0.01 M weak acid $\mathrm{HX}\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=4 \times 10^{-10}\right)$ is found to be 5. Now the acid solution is diluted with excess of water so that the pH of the solution changes to 6. The new concentration of the diluted weak acid is given as $x \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$. The value of x is ________ (nearest integer)
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \quad \mathrm{HX}_{(\mathrm{aq)}} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{X}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} \quad \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=4 \times 10^{-10} \\
& 0.01(1-\alpha) \quad 0.01 \alpha \quad 0.01 \alpha \quad \text { Not justified } \\
& \Rightarrow 0.01 \alpha=10^{-5} \Rightarrow \alpha=10^{-3} \\
& \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}}=0.01 \alpha^2=10^{-8}
\end{aligned}$
On dilution let final concentration of $\mathrm{HX}=\mathrm{c} \mathrm{M}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Hx}_{(\mathrm{an})} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+\mathrm{X}_{(\mathrm{an})}^{-} \\
& \mathrm{C}(1-\alpha) \quad \mathrm{C} \alpha \quad \mathrm{C} \alpha \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{C} \alpha=10^{-6}...(1) \\
& \quad \frac{\mathrm{C} \alpha^{2}}{1-\alpha}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=10^{-8}...(2) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{10^{-6} \alpha}{1-\alpha}=10^{-8}
\end{aligned}$
Data given is inconsistent \& contradictory. This should be bonus.

subject: Chemistry
q: The $\mathrm{pKa}$ of a weak acid $(\mathrm{HA})$ is $4.5$. The $\mathrm{pOH}$ of an aqueous buffered solution of $\mathrm{HA}$ in which $50 \%$ of the acid is ionized is
question: The $\mathrm{pKa}$ of a weak acid $(\mathrm{HA})$ is $4.5$. The $\mathrm{pOH}$ of an aqueous buffered solution of $\mathrm{HA}$ in which $50 \%$ of the acid is ionized is
solution:
For buffer solution
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}+\log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]} \\
& =4.5+\log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]}
\end{aligned}$
as $\mathrm{HA}$ is $50 \%$ ionized so $[\text{Salt}]$=$[\text{Acid}]$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{pH}=4.5 \\
& \mathrm{pH}+\mathrm{pOH}=14 \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{pOH}=14-4.5=9.5
\end{aligned}$
Hence (C) is correct.

q: When rain is accompanied by a thunderstorm, the collected rain water will have a $\mathrm{pH}$ value
question: When rain is accompanied by a thunderstorm, the collected rain water will have a $\mathrm{pH}$ value
options: slightly higher than that when the thunderstorm is not there
which depends on the amount of dust in air
slightly lower than that of rain water without thunderstorm
solution: The rain water after thunderstorm contains dissolved acid and therefore the $\mathrm{pH}$ is less than rain water without thunderstorm.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The pH of a solution obtained by mixing $5$ mL of $0.1$ M $NH_4OH$ solution with $250$ mL of $0.1$ M $NH_4Cl$ solution is _____ $\times 10^{-2}$. (Nearest integer)
Given: $pK_b(NH_4OH) = 4.74$
$\log 2 = 0.30$
$\log 3 = 0.48$
$\log 5 = 0.70$
question:
The pH of a solution obtained by mixing $5$ mL of $0.1$ M $NH_4OH$ solution with $250$ mL of $0.1$ M $NH_4Cl$ solution is _____ $\times 10^{-2}$. (Nearest integer)
Given: $pK_b(NH_4OH) = 4.74$
$\log 2 = 0.30$
$\log 3 = 0.48$
$\log 5 = 0.70$
solution:
The given mixture forms a basic buffer solution.

Millimoles of base $NH_4OH = 5 \times 0.1 = 0.5$ mmol

Millimoles of salt $NH_4Cl = 250 \times 0.1 = 25$ mmol

Using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for a basic buffer:

$pOH = pK_b + \log \left( \dfrac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Base}]} \right)$

$pOH = 4.74 + \log \left( \dfrac{25}{0.5} \right)$

$pOH = 4.74 + \log (50)$

We know that $\log (50) = \log (5 \times 10) = \log 5 + \log 10 = 0.70 + 1 = 1.70$

$pOH = 4.74 + 1.70 = 6.44$

The pH of the solution is given by:

$pH = 14 - pOH$

$pH = 14 - 6.44 = 7.56$

$pH = 756 \times 10^{-2}$

Answer: $756$

subject: Chemistry
q: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> at which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>OH</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>11</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> begins to precipitate from a solution containing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Mg</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> ions is ______.
question: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> at which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>OH</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi>sp</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>11</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> begins to precipitate from a solution containing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Mg</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> ions is ______.
solution:
Precipitation when Q=Ksp

Q=Reaction quotient, it is the ratio of products to reactants at any time of reaction.

Ksp=Solubility product.

MgOH2⇋Mg2++2OH-

Mg2+OH-2=10-11

0.1×OH-2=10-11⇒OH-=10-5

⇒pOH=5  ⇒pH=14-5=9

question:
50 mL of 0.1 M CH3COOH is being titrated against 0.1 M NaOH. When 25 mL of NaOH has been added, the pH of the solution will be____×10-2. (Nearest integer)

(Given : pKaCH3COOH=4.76)

log2=0.30

log7=0.84

solution:
CH3COOHaq+NaOHaq.→CH3COONaaq+H2Ol

5 mmole              2.5 mmoles                                                     initially2.5 m moles            0                           2.5 mmoles              after reaction

Resultant solution is acidic buffer solution with same concentration of acid and salts.

pH = pKa + log[salt][acid]

So, pH of solution pH=pKa=4.76=476×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q:
The solubility product of BaSO4 is 1×10-10 at 298 K. The solubility of BaSO4 in 0.1M K2SO4(aq) solution is    ------×10-9 g L-1(nearest integer).
Given: Molar mass of BaSO4 is 233 g mol-1
question:
The solubility product of BaSO4 is 1×10-10 at 298 K. The solubility of BaSO4 in 0.1M K2SO4(aq) solution is    ------×10-9 g L-1(nearest integer).
Given: Molar mass of BaSO4 is 233 g mol-1
solution:
For the insoluble salt barium sulphate, sulphate ion added is the common ion. Due to the common ion effect the solubility of barium sulphate decreases. Let us assume solubility of barium sulphate is S.

BaSO4s⇌Ba2+Saq+SO42-S+0.1aq
 K2SO40.1-0.1⟶ K+0.1aq+SO42-0.1aq
KSP=Ba2+SO42-
1×10-10=S(S+0.1)
≃0.1 S
S=10-9M=233×10-9gL-1

subject: Chemistry
q: $\mathrm{K}_{\text {sp }}$ for $\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_3$ is $1.6 \times 10^{-30}$. What is the molar solubility of this salt in water?
question: $\mathrm{K}_{\text {sp }}$ for $\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_3$ is $1.6 \times 10^{-30}$. What is the molar solubility of this salt in water?
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{OH})_3 \rightleftharpoons \underset{ \mathrm{s}}{\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}}(\mathrm{aq})+\underset{3 \mathrm{~s}}{3 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})} \\ & \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\mathrm{s}(3 \mathrm{~s})^3 \\ & 1.6 \times 10^{-30}=27 \mathrm{~s}^4 \\ & \sqrt[4]{\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-30}}{27}}=\mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: Only litre buffer solution was prepared by adding 0.10 mol each of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ and $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}$ in deionised water. The change in pH on addition of 0.05 mol of HCl to the above solution is ________ $\times 10^{-2}$, (Nearest integer) (Given : $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of $\mathrm{NH}_3=4.745$ and $\log _{10} 3=0.477$)
question: Only litre buffer solution was prepared by adding 0.10 mol each of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ and $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}$ in deionised water. The change in pH on addition of 0.05 mol of HCl to the above solution is ________ $\times 10^{-2}$, (Nearest integer) (Given : $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}$ of $\mathrm{NH}_3=4.745$ and $\log _{10} 3=0.477$)
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}+\log \frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_3\right]} \\
& \mathbf{p O H}=4.745
\end{aligned}$
on adding 0.05 mole HCl
$\begin{aligned}
& \begin{array}{llll}
\mathrm{NH}_3+ & \mathrm{H}^{\oplus} & \rightarrow & \mathrm{NH}_4^{\oplus} \\
0.1 & 0.05 & & 0.1 \\
0.05 & 0 & & 0.15
\end{array} \\
& \mathrm{pOH}^{\prime}=4.745+\log 3 \\
& \mathrm{pOH}^{\prime}-\mathrm{pOH}=0.477 \\
& 14-\mathrm{pH}^{\prime}-14+\mathrm{pH}=0.477 \\
& \Delta \mathrm{pH}=0.477 \\
& =47.7 \times 10^{-2} \approx 48 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}$
